# UC-19.1.36 · Service Profile vNIC Redundancy and Failover Audit (Cisco UCS)

## Description

UC-19.1.2 covers compliance posture; UC-19.1.11 covers live association failures; UC-19.1.36 is the topology gap that survives a clean compliance check. The saved search is the technical mechanism: it left-joins a non-template lsServer sp_dn list, aggregates vnicEther with switchId coalesced to lower-case, joins vnicEtherIf on the parent dn for failoverEnabled, and counts only operable operState values. It classifies each profile into single_fabric_a_only, single_fabric_b_only, both_fabrics_no_failover, redundant_with_failover, or unknown_topology, then maps severity_bucket so operators see A/B gaps and missing automatic failover that a clean configState/assocState matrix in UC-19.1.2 can still pass.

## Value

UC-19.1.2 covers compliance posture; UC-19.1.11 covers live association failures; UC-19.1.36 is the topology gap that survives a clean compliance check. A profile can be Compliant in UC-19.1.2 yet still leave every production vNIC on one switchId with vnicEtherIf.failoverEnabled false, so a single Fabric Interconnect or upstream fault darkens a healthy blade the same way a full outage would. The bridge class here is not “association failed this minute” and not “uplink LAG is down” — it is configuration drift the compliance matrix can miss. The storage or cluster team is not the first call: compute platform must correct profile design, template sprawl, or a MAC pool that accidentally pinned both paths to the same side before the next one-sided fabric fault takes a healthy host offline.

## Implementation

Enable **`cisco:ucs:inventory`** in **`Splunk_TA_cisco_ucs`** and confirm `vnicEther` + `vnicEtherIf` + `lsServer` in the modular input class set. Save the **`spl`**, schedule a **30-minute** (or **hourly**) report over the **-24h@h** window, and route **`severity_bucket=critical`/`warning`** to the **UCSM / data-centre network platform** team with throttle on **`sp_dn`**, not a raw **NOC** blast. Optional: add the **`cisco_ucs_single_nic_appliances`** `lookup` from **detailedImplementation** Step 2 to downgrade known single-path designs with **CMDB** provenance.

## Detailed Implementation

**Prerequisites**
• UC-19.1.2 covers compliance posture; UC-19.1.11 covers live association failures; UC-19.1.36 is the topology gap that survives a clean compliance check.
• Install Splunk_TA_cisco_ucs (Splunkbase 2731) on a Heavy Forwarder and on Search Heads; the role must search the cisco_ucs index. Outbound HTTPS 443 to the UCSM virtual IP, TLS trust in place, and a read-only API user with rights to the org, ls-*, and ether- subtrees your estate uses. Import the full certificate chain; do not use personal admin credentials.

**Sibling differentiation**
UC-19.1.2 is the scheduled lsServer assocState and configState matrix. UC-19.1.11 is the live pipeline where association or apply is failing or inaccessible right now. UC-19.1.5 is the Northbound Ethernet LAN LAG and fabricEthLanPc health test. This use case, UC-19.1.36, is the Southbound vNIC A/B and failover posture audit: it can be green in 19.1.2 and 19.1.5 while a profile still has every operable vNIC on one side.

**Step 1 — Configure data collection**
(1) Copy $SPLUNK_HOME/etc/apps/Splunk_TA_cisco_ucs/default/inputs.conf to local/inputs.conf and enable the **Cisco UCS inventory** modular input the vendor documents for your release. Set disabled=0, url=https://<ucsm-vip>, the credential, index=cisco_ucs, and keep sourcetype cisco:ucs:inventory. Stanza names differ by add-on build—mirror the exact stanza from default/inputs.conf, not a hand-invented label. A placeholder shape:

```ini
# [vendor inventory stanza from default/inputs.conf]
disabled = 0
url = https://<ucsm-vip>
index = cisco_ucs
interval = 60
# vnicEther, vnicEtherIf, and lsServer must appear in the class list your TA exposes
```

(2) In the add-on’s advanced or class-list surface for the XML inventory poller, ensure vnicEther, vnicEtherIf, and lsServer are not removed to save license. This use case is blind if any of those are dropped.

(3) **Post-poll validation** in Search, in order: `index=cisco_ucs | stats count by sourcetype` (expect cisco:ucs:inventory); `index=cisco_ucs sourcetype="cisco:ucs:inventory" earliest=-24h@h | stats count by classId | sort - count` (non-zero for vnicEther, vnicEtherIf, and lsServer where servers exist); and `| head 1` samples with fieldsummary on a vnicEther row to confirm switchId vs switch_id and operState spellings. Never substitute a non-existent cisco:ucs:config feed—use inventory or fix ingestion.

(4) **Hardware note:** B200 M5 versus M6/M7 blades, X-series with X9100 generation IOMs, and 2204/2208/2304/2408 line-ups can change when vnic Ether objects first show in XML after a chassis or IOM event. A 30-minute alert cadence matches inventory polling; do not over-page during a single re-discovery poll.

**Step 2 — Create the search and the optional single-NIC exception lookup**
(1) Save the use-case spl as a report named uc_19_1_36_vnic_ab_posture, then promote an alert after optional lookup wiring that fires on severity_bucket in (warning, critical) with throttle on sp_dn.

(2) For intentional single-vNIC or single-fabric designs (in-line security appliances, CMDB-tagged test blades), add the UTF-8 CSV cisco_ucs_single_nic_appliances.csv to your private app with columns org, sp_name, exception_reason, owner, expiry_date. In transforms.conf:

```
[cisco_ucs_single_nic_appliances]
filename = cisco_ucs_single_nic_appliances.csv
```

After reload, you can join before paging:

```spl
| lookup local=true cisco_ucs_single_nic_appliances org sp_name OUTPUT exception_reason, owner, expiry_date
| where isnull(expiry_date) OR strptime(expiry_date, "%Y-%m-%d") > now()
```

Add a private eval to downgrade or drop rows when exception_reason and owner are present and the expiry is in the future. Tighten the time parse for your locale if needed.

(3) The lookup is governance, not a substitute for good data: keep owner and expiry in ITGC-style change evidence.

**Step 3 — Validate against UCS Manager (GUI and truth)**
(1) From a critical or warning row, copy sp_dn. In UCSM go to Servers, Service Profiles, and match the org path. Open the profile Network (or the vNIC list for your 4.2/4.3 UI).

(2) For each vnicEther row, read switchId as A or B. Compare the GUI’s fabric placement to the Splunk counts of operable vNICs on A versus B after one inventory cycle. The search lowercases the same field the TA might expose as switch_id or switch.

(3) For vnicEtherIf, the Failover (or active-active) flag maps to failoverEnabled. If the GUI shows true but Splunk’s failover_enabled_count is zero, vnicEtherIf is probably missing from the class list or blocked by API scope, not a moral defect of the app owner.

(4) If operState does not match, check IOM health, firmware activation, and M6/M7 discovery quirks. vnicConnDef is policy intent only until you see a vnicEther in inventory; otherwise unknown_topology is the honest class.

(5) When GUI and search disagree, compare poll time, UCSM high-availability cutover, and the read-only user’s org scope. GUI admins with broader rights than the poller is a common split-brain.

**Step 4 — Operationalize (dashboard, alert, routing)**
(1) Dashboard: row 1 single value of critical+warning; row 2 the table from the spl; row 3 a bar of redundancy_state by org. Keep UC-19.1.2 and UC-19.1.5 on the same page: healthy Northbound LAGs from 19.1.5 do not prove 19.1.36 is green for server-facing vNICs.

(2) **Alert schedule:** 30 minutes or hourly for a posture audit—not a five-minute NOC cadence. Inventory lags a poll cycle by design.

(3) Throttle on sp_dn. Route to the UCSM or compute platform team that owns templates and vNIC policy; the raw NOC queue is the wrong first hop for a configuration drift that is not yet a live outage unless you add a cisco:ucs:faults companion panel for adapter faults.

(4) **Evidence:** weekly CSV export to the same restricted share as your other posture evidence; name files with the UCSM cluster FQDN in multi-site estates.

**Step 5 — Tuning and troubleshooting (gotchas)**
1. M5 versus M6/M7 and chassis vNIC discovery: an IOM swap or FEX reseat can make vnicEther rows lag one poll; widen a one-off ad-hoc search to -4h before you rebind vNICs in UCSM. Do not conflate vnicConnDef (definition only) with vnicEther (instantiation).

2. Static versus dynamic MAC pool side effects: operable_total may be wrong for reasons unrelated to A/B; fix pools in UCSM, but the A/B split in this use case is still the right test once addresses are valid.

3. When vnicEtherIf rows are missing for a real vnicEther, failover_enabled_count stays zero and the row drifts to both_fabrics_no_failover; that is a class coverage bug to fix in the modular input, not a Nexus vPC problem—compare UC-19.1.5.

4. Intersight-converted profiles that still render in UCSM can hide or reshape failover in ways this UCSM-only search does not model; the exclusions field applies—treat a sudden drop in cisco:ucs:inventory for a whole domain as a migration signal, not a green scorecard.

5. B200 M5 lab blades versus M7 production: use org-level suppression for fault-inject labs, not a global product mute.

6. Single-fabric A or B only after a template clone error: the single_fabric_a_only and single_fabric_b_only bucket is a critical finding in production org paths; use the org field or prefix, not a global dev rule.

7. Approved UCSM maintenance that legitimately places both temporary vNICs on one fabric: bind suppression to the change record on sp_dn for that window, not a blind alert disable.

8. Inventory poll the same second as an UCSM cluster failover: one odd empty read is not enough to RMA a Fabric Interconnect. Compare UC-19.1.5 for Northbound health; a healthy fabricEthLanPc with 0/0 A/B vNIC counts in this use case is exactly the “uplinks fine, host face wrong” pattern this UC is written to catch.

**Close-out checklist**
• | stats by sourcetype,classId shows cisco:ucs:inventory with vnicEther, vnicEtherIf, and lsServer in non-zero counts where you have servers.
• One lab test profile is intentionally mis-pinned to prove single_fabric_*_only fires and clears after correction.
• Optional appliance lookup is reviewed for owner and expiry.
• Alert throttles on sp_dn and routes to the platform team.
• UC-19.1.2, UC-19.1.5, and UC-19.1.11 panels stay adjacent for triage.
Additional operator notes (read once before production paging):
The inventory subsearch in the primary SPL intentionally keeps both lsserver and vnicether rows long enough to compute sp_tpl_max so template-derived names under initial-template and updating-template do not pollute the A/B test. If your estate never indexes lsServer in the same 24h window, inner joins would drop vNIC rows—verify lsServer and vnicEther both appear in the class list. When both sides have operable vNICs and failover is administratively set to a manual fail-over design (rare, but some regulated environments require it), the row lands in both_fabrics_no_failover on purpose: that is a policy conversation, not a false negative in this use case. When you add the optional cisco_ucs_single_nic_appliances lookup, keep the CSV in the same private app that owns your transforms.conf so AppInspect and bundle promotion stay simple; never ship customer-specific exceptions in the public library JSON without a redacted example row.

Cross-checks that save bridge time: if UC-19.1.5 shows a degraded fabric Eth LAN port-channel in the same minute this search shows redundant_with_failover, the server-facing story can still be correct—Northbound and Southbound are independent. If UC-19.1.11 is firing for the same sp_dn, fix association and apply first; 19.1.36 is not the primary on-call for a failed lsServer state until the server is actually associated and carrying workload again.


## SPL

```spl
index=cisco_ucs sourcetype="cisco:ucs:inventory" earliest=-24h@h
| eval cidl=lower(coalesce(classId, class_id, ""))
| where cidl="lsserver" AND !match(lower(coalesce(type, "")), "template$")
| eval sp_dn=dn
| eval org=replace(sp_dn, "^org-root/(.+)/ls-[^/]+$", "\1")
| eval sp_name=replace(sp_dn, "^.+/ls-([^/]+)$", "\1")
| join type=left sp_dn
    [ search index=cisco_ucs sourcetype="cisco:ucs:inventory" earliest=-24h@h
      | eval cidl=lower(coalesce(classId, class_id, ""))
      | where cidl IN ("vnicether", "lsserver")
      | rex field=dn max_match=0 "^(?<sp_dn>org-root/.+?/ls-[^/]+)(?:/ether-|$)"
      | eval sp_tpl=if(cidl="lsserver" AND match(lower(coalesce(type, "")), "template$"), 1, 0)
      | eventstats max(sp_tpl) as sp_tpl_max by sp_dn
      | where cidl="vnicether" AND (sp_tpl_max=0 OR isnull(sp_tpl_max))
      | eval vnic_ether_dn=dn
      | eval sw=lower(trim(coalesce(switchId, switch_id, switch, "")))
      | eval oper=lower(trim(coalesce(operState, oper_state, "")))
      | eval is_op=if(oper IN ("operable", "up", "online"), 1, 0)
      | join type=left vnic_ether_dn
          [ search index=cisco_ucs sourcetype="cisco:ucs:inventory" earliest=-24h@h
            | eval cidl=lower(coalesce(classId, class_id, ""))
            | where cidl="vnicetherif"
            | eval parent=replace(dn, "/[^/]+$", "")
            | eval fe=lower(toString(coalesce(failoverEnabled, failover_enabled, "")))
            | eval vnic_has_fe=if(fe IN ("true", "1", "yes", "t"), 1, 0)
            | stats max(vnic_has_fe) as vnic_has_fe by parent
            | rename parent as vnic_ether_dn
          ]
      | fillnull value=0 vnic_has_fe
      | eval cnt_a=if(sw="a" AND is_op=1, 1, 0)
      | eval cnt_b=if(sw="b" AND is_op=1, 1, 0)
      | eval fe_hit=if(vnic_has_fe=1, 1, 0)
      | stats sum(cnt_a) as vnic_count_a, sum(cnt_b) as vnic_count_b, sum(eval(if(is_op=1, 1, 0))) as operable_total, sum(fe_hit) as failover_enabled_count by sp_dn
    ]
| fillnull value=0 vnic_count_a vnic_count_b operable_total failover_enabled_count
| eval redundancy_state=case(
    vnic_count_a >= 1 AND vnic_count_b=0, "single_fabric_a_only",
    vnic_count_b >= 1 AND vnic_count_a=0, "single_fabric_b_only",
    vnic_count_a >= 1 AND vnic_count_b >= 1 AND failover_enabled_count=0, "both_fabrics_no_failover",
    vnic_count_a >= 1 AND vnic_count_b >= 1 AND failover_enabled_count>0, "redundant_with_failover",
    vnic_count_a=0 AND vnic_count_b=0, "unknown_topology",
    1=1, "unknown_topology"
  )
| eval severity_bucket=case(
    redundancy_state IN ("single_fabric_a_only", "single_fabric_b_only"), "critical",
    redundancy_state="both_fabrics_no_failover", "warning",
    redundancy_state="redundant_with_failover", "ok",
    1=1, "info"
  )
| eval sev_key=case(severity_bucket="critical", 1, severity_bucket="warning", 2, severity_bucket="ok", 3, 1=1, 4)
| table sp_dn, org, sp_name, vnic_count_a, vnic_count_b, operable_total, failover_enabled_count, redundancy_state, severity_bucket, sev_key
| sort 0 sev_key sp_name
| fields - sev_key
```

## Visualization

(1) Table sorted by the internal severity key: **`sp_dn`**, `org`, `sp_name`, **`vnic_count_a`**, **`vnic_count_b`**, `operable_total`, **`failover_enabled_count`**, `redundancy_state`, `severity_bucket`. (2) **Single value** by **`severity_bucket`** (critical|warning|ok|info) with colour thresholds. (3) Stacked bar or column: count of `redundancy_state` per `org` (first path segment) for quarterly hygiene. (4) Row beneath **UC-19.1.2** and **UC-19.1.5** on the same dashboard: caption explains cross-check—healthy uplink LAGs do not prove A/B vNIC use.
